title: SpecCheck: Detecting LLM Hallucinations by Testing Confidence Monotonicity Across Specificity Levels
description: We propose SpecCheck, a training-free framework for detecting hallucinations in LLM-generated text by probing the model's confidence in the same claim expressed at multiple levels of specificity. For each atomic claim, we generate a 'specificity ladder' ranging from the original specific claim to increasingly abstract versions, then test whether confidence increases monotonically with abstraction. Violations of this monotonicity signal hallucination, and the level where monotonicity breaks identifies the granularity at which fabrication occurs.
motivation: Current hallucination detection methods (SelfCheckGPT, semantic entropy, FActScore) evaluate claims at a single level of specificity—they check if a specific claim is consistent across samples or supported by external knowledge. However, LLMs frequently hallucinate specific details (exact dates, numbers, names) while retaining approximately correct general knowledge. No existing method exploits this specificity structure. By probing the same claim at multiple granularities, we can detect hallucinations that are invisible to single-granularity methods and additionally diagnose *which level of detail* is unreliable.
proposed_approach: SpecCheck operates in four stages: (1) Decompose LLM output into atomic claims following FActScore's protocol. (2) For each claim, generate K versions at decreasing specificity levels using LLM-based or template-based abstraction (original → approximate → categorical → abstract). (3) Estimate the model's confidence at each level via sampling-based consistency or log-probabilities. (4) Detect hallucinations by checking for violations of confidence monotonicity—confidence should increase or stay constant as specificity decreases. We also compute a Hallucination Granularity Index to identify the level of detail at which fabrication occurs. The approach is training-free, requires no external knowledge base, and works with any LLM.

related_work.title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
related_work.authors: Sewon Min, Kalpesh Krishna, Xinxi Lyu, Mike Lewis, Wen-tau Yih, Pang Wei Koh, Mohit Iyyer, Luke Zettlemoyer, Hannaneh Hajishirzi
related_work.relation: Pioneered atomic claim decomposition for factuality evaluation. SpecCheck builds on this decomposition but adds multi-granularity probing instead of external verification.

related_work.title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Potsawee Manakul, Adian Liusie, Mark J.F. Gales
related_work.relation: Uses cross-sample consistency for hallucination detection. SpecCheck differs by probing the same claim at different specificity levels rather than comparing different complete responses.

related_work.title: Detecting hallucinations in large language models using semantic entropy
related_work.authors: Sebastian Farquhar, Jannik Kossen, Lorenz Kuhn, Yarin Gal
related_work.relation: Computes entropy over semantically equivalent outputs for uncertainty estimation. SpecCheck instead examines how uncertainty varies across specificity levels for the same underlying fact.

related_work.title: Long-form factuality in large language models (SAFE)
related_work.authors: Jerry Wei, Chengrun Yang, Xinying Song, Yifeng Lu, Nathan Hu, et al.
related_work.relation: Search-augmented factuality evaluator that verifies atomic claims via Google Search. SpecCheck provides a search-free alternative using model self-consistency across granularities.

related_work.title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
related_work.authors: Stephanie Lin, Jacob Hilton, Owain Evans
related_work.relation: Benchmark for evaluating truthfulness. We use TruthfulQA as one of our evaluation datasets.

related_work.title: Take a Step Back: Evoking Reasoning via Abstraction in Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Huaixiu Steven Zheng, Swaroop Mishra, Xinyun Chen, Heng-Tze Cheng, Ed H. Chi, Quoc V. Le, Denny Zhou
related_work.relation: Uses abstraction to improve reasoning by first answering a more general question. SpecCheck applies abstraction for detection rather than generation, and does so systematically across multiple levels.

related_work.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
related_work.authors: Xuezhi Wang, Jason Wei, Dale Schuurmans, Quoc V. Le, Ed H. Chi, Sharan Narang, Aakanksha Chowdhery, Denny Zhou
related_work.relation: Foundational work on using sampling consistency as a signal. SpecCheck extends this idea from answer-level to claim-level multi-granularity consistency.

related_work.title: Semantic Entropy Probes: Robust and Cheap Hallucination Detection in LLMs
related_work.authors: Jannik Kossen, Jiatong Han, Lorenz Kuhn, Yarin Gal, Sebastian Farquhar
related_work.relation: Lightweight probes that approximate semantic entropy from hidden states. SpecCheck is complementary—it uses behavioral probing rather than internal state analysis.

hypothesis: Claims for which an LLM's confidence does not increase monotonically as the claim is made more abstract are significantly more likely to be hallucinated than claims exhibiting monotonic confidence increase. Specifically, we hypothesize that (1) >85% of verified-true claims exhibit confidence monotonicity across a 4-level specificity ladder, while <60% of hallucinated claims do, and (2) the specificity level at which monotonicity first breaks correctly identifies the granularity of hallucination in >70% of cases.
success_criteria: SpecCheck achieves statistically significantly higher AUC-PR than SelfCheckGPT and verbalized confidence baselines on at least 2 of 3 benchmarks (FActScore biographies, LongFact, TruthfulQA)
Confidence monotonicity holds for >85% of verified-true claims across all tested models
Confidence monotonicity is violated for >40% of hallucinated claims
The Hallucination Granularity Index correctly identifies the level of fabrication in >70% of analyzed cases
SpecCheck provides complementary signal to existing methods, improving detection when combined
